channel.title: Marxism on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Marxism on emsenn.net

item.title: Reification
item.description:
Reification is the transformation of historically produced social relations into apparently natural or thing-like facts.

In Marxist and Lukácsian analysis, reification obscures mediation: what is socially produced appears as if it were simply given, autonomous, and beyond alteration [@lukacs-sep-2023].

The critical task is de-reification: recovering the processes, relations, and institutions that produce the appearance of objectivity.

Related terms

  • Totality
  • Dialectics
  • Historicization

item.title: Uneven and Combined Development
item.description:
Uneven and combined development names a historical pattern in which different developmental tempos coexist and interpenetrate, producing hybrid social forms rather than linear stage succession [@trotsky-hrr-1930].

In contemporary theory, it functions as a general historical-dialectical category for relational development under asymmetry [@rosenberg-ucd-2013].

Related terms

  • Historicization
  • Totality
  • History of Dialectics

item.title: Class Struggle
item.description: Class struggle is the conflict between social classes whose interests are structurally opposed within a given mode of production. Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels placed it at the center of historical analysis: the history of all existing society, in their formulation, is the history of class struggles — freeman and slave, patrician and plebeian, lord and serf, bourgeoisie and proletariat. Each pairing names a relation of exploitation in which one class appropriates the labor of another.

item.title: Historical Materialism
item.description:
Historical materialism is the method Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels developed for analyzing human societies. Its central claim: the way a society organizes the production of its material life — who works, with what tools, under what relations of ownership — determines the general character of its social, political, and intellectual processes. The economic structure (the “base”) shapes the legal, political, religious, and cultural institutions (the “superstructure”) that arise from it.

The base-superstructure model operates through a specific mechanism. As productive forces (technology, knowledge, labor power) develop, they come into conflict with the existing relations of production (property relations, class structure). This contradiction generates class struggle, which drives historical transformation. Feudalism gives way to capitalism not through the progress of ideas but through the material inability of feudal relations to contain the productive forces developing within them. Marx argued that capitalism would face the same fate: the contradiction between socialized production and private appropriation would generate the conditions for communism.

item.title: Primitive Accumulation
item.description: Primitive accumulation is Karl Marx’s term for the historical processes that created the preconditions for capitalist production: the separation of people from the means of their own subsistence. In England, this took the form of enclosures — the forcible conversion of common land into private property, which dispossessed peasants and created a class of people with nothing to sell but their labor power. Globally, it took the form of colonial conquest, the Atlantic slave trade, and the extraction of wealth from colonized peoples and lands. Marx treated these as the “original sin” of capitalism: the violence that preceded and enabled the apparently peaceful exchange of wages for labor.

item.title: Surplus Value
item.description: Surplus value is Karl Marx’s term for the difference between the value a worker produces during the labor process and the value they receive as wages. A worker employed for eight hours may produce enough value to cover their wage in four hours; the remaining four hours of labor produce value that the capitalist appropriates as profit. This appropriation is not theft in the legal sense — the worker has agreed to sell their labor power — but it is exploitation in the structural sense: the entire arrangement is organized to extract unpaid labor.

item.title: Vanguardism
item.description: Vanguardism is the theory that revolutionary transformation requires a disciplined party of professional revolutionaries who possess theoretical clarity about historical conditions and can lead the working class from “trade union consciousness” (concern with immediate economic demands) to revolutionary consciousness (the understanding that the entire system must be overthrown). Vladimir Lenin articulated this position in What Is to Be Done? (1902), arguing that workers, left to their own devices, would develop only economic demands rather than political ones, and that a party organized on principles of democratic centralism was necessary to direct revolutionary struggle.

item.title: Exploring the semantics of The Late Marx citers
item.description:
import csv, json, re, time, random
import requests

OUTFILE = "abstracts.csv"
CONTACT_EMAIL = "emsenn@emsenn.net"
MAX_RESULTS = 200
SLEEP = 1.2
SEED = 42
random.seed(SEED)

QUERY_ANDERSON = '"Kevin B. Anderson" "The Late Marx and the Russian Road"'
QUERY_CONTROL  = 'Marxism OR "Karl Marx"'
MARX_FILTER = re.compile(r"(marx|marxism|historical materialism|capitalis\w*|communis\w*|class struggle)", re.I)
BAD_SUBJECTS = {"chemistry", "physics", "materials", "engineering", "medicine", "biology", "geology"}

def query_crossref(query, rows=100, cursor="*"):
    url = "https://api.crossref.org/works"
    params = {"query.bibliographic": query, "rows": rows, "cursor": cursor, "mailto": CONTACT_EMAIL}
    while True:
        r = requests.get(url, params=params, timeout=30)
        if r.status_code != 200:
            print("Error", r.status_code, r.text)
            break
        data = r.json()
        items = data["message"]["items"]
        for it in items:
            yield it
        cursor_next = data["message"].get("next-cursor")
        if not cursor_next or len(items) == 0:
            break
        params["cursor"] = cursor_next
        time.sleep(SLEEP)

def clean(text):
    if not text:
        return ""
    return re.sub(r"\s+", " ", str(text)).strip()

def extract(it):
    doi = it.get("DOI", "")
    title = clean(" ".join(it.get("title", [])))
    abstract = clean(it.get("abstract", ""))
    year = None
    for fld in ("published-print","published-online","created"):
        if fld in it and "date-parts" in it[fld]:
            year = it[fld]["date-parts"][0][0]
            break
    authors = ", ".join([a.get("family","") for a in it.get("author",[]) if "family" in a])
    publisher = it.get("publisher", "")
    subjects = [s.lower() for s in it.get("subject", [])]
    lang = it.get("language","").lower() if it.get("language") else ""
    return {"doi":doi,"title":title,"abstract":abstract,"year":year,
            "authors":authors,"publisher":publisher,"subjects":subjects,"lang":lang}

def is_marxist(rec):
    """Heuristic: English + humanities/social + Marx lexicon"""
    if rec["lang"] and not rec["lang"].startswith("en"):
        return False
    if any(bad in rec["subjects"] for bad in BAD_SUBJECTS):
        return False
    text = f"{rec['title']} {rec['abstract']}".lower()
    return bool(MARX_FILTER.search(text))

# --- Main collection ---
def collect(query, label):
    records = []
    for item in query_crossref(query, rows=100):
        rec = extract(item)
        if not is_marxist(rec):
            continue
        rec["label"] = label
        records.append(rec)
        if len(records) >= MAX_RESULTS:
            break
    return records

print("Fetching Anderson-related records…")
anderson_records = collect(QUERY_ANDERSON, "cites_anderson")

print("Fetching Marxist control records…")
control_records = collect(QUERY_CONTROL, "control")

rows = anderson_records + control_records
random.shuffle(rows)

# --- Write CSV ---
with open(OUTFILE, "w", newline="", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    writer = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=[
        "label","year","title","authors","publisher","doi","abstract"
    ])
    writer.writeheader()
    for r in rows:
        writer.writerow({k:r.get(k,"") for k in writer.fieldnames})

print(f"Wrote {len(rows)} records to {OUTFILE}")
print("Sample titles:")
for r in rows[:10]:
    print(f"- [{r['label']}] {r['year']} {r['title'][:80]}…")

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
explore-anderson-citations.py
---------------------------------
Quick analysis of whether Anderson-citing works use more 'moral' or 'method'
language, using a simple keyword frequency approach on abstracts.csv.

Input CSV must contain columns: label, text (abstract or concatenated text).
Label values: 'cites_anderson' or 'control'.

Outputs group means and 95% confidence intervals per 1000 words.
"""

import re, csv, statistics as stats
from collections import Counter

# --- Keyword patterns ---
METHOD = r"\b(method|methods|measurement|data(set)?|survey|fieldwork|interview|sampling|regress\w*|model\w*|estimate\w*|coding|reliab\w*|validi\w*|operationali\w*|variable\w*|hypothes\w*|test(ed|ing)?|significan\w*|simulat\w*|network\w*|experiment\w*|appendix|replicat\w*|code|archive|corpus|algorithm)\b"
MORAL  = r"\b(redempt\w*|inclusion|inclusive|recognit\w*|plural\w*|eurocentr\w*|decolonial\w*|coloniality|humanism|ethic(s|al)\b(?!\s*protocol)|empath\w*|conscience|guilt|atone\w*|moral\w*|legitim\w*|rehabilitat\w*|reconciliat\w*|solidarit\w*|utopian\w*|hopeful|hope|salvat\w*|justice|injustic\w*|violenc\w*|suffer\w*|oppress\w*|emancipat\w*|dignit\w*|liberat\w*|redress|repair|healing)\b"

METHOD_RE = re.compile(METHOD, re.I)
MORAL_RE  = re.compile(MORAL, re.I)

def normalize(text):
    if not text:
        return ""
    return re.sub(r"\s+", " ", text).lower()

def count_keywords(text, pattern):
    return len(pattern.findall(text))

def load_records(path="abstracts.csv"):
    records = []
    with open(path, newline="", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        reader = csv.DictReader(f)
        for row in reader:
            label = row.get("label","").strip()
            # Concatenate title + abstract for analysis
            text = normalize((row.get("title","") or "") + " " + (row.get("abstract","") or ""))
            if not text:
                continue
            records.append({"label":label, "text":text})
    return records

def compute_scores(records):
    results = []
    for rec in records:
        words = rec["text"].split()
        n_words = len(words) if words else 1
        method_count = count_keywords(rec["text"], METHOD_RE)
        moral_count  = count_keywords(rec["text"], MORAL_RE)
        # Per 1000 words
        mth_per_1k = method_count * 1000.0 / n_words
        mor_per_1k = moral_count * 1000.0 / n_words
        results.append({
            "label": rec["label"],
            "method_per_1k": mth_per_1k,
            "moral_per_1k": mor_per_1k
        })
    return results

def group_stats(results):
    groups = {}
    for r in results:
        groups.setdefault(r["label"], {"method":[], "moral":[]})
        groups[r["label"]]["method"].append(r["method_per_1k"])
        groups[r["label"]]["moral"].append(r["moral_per_1k"])
    return groups

def summarize(vals):
    if not vals:
        return (0.0,0.0,0.0)
    mean = stats.mean(vals)
    if len(vals) > 1:
        stdev = stats.pstdev(vals)
        # 95% CI ~ mean ± 1.96 * (stdev / sqrt(n))
        se = stdev / (len(vals) ** 0.5)
        ci_low = mean - 1.96 * se
        ci_high = mean + 1.96 * se
    else:
        ci_low = ci_high = mean
    return (mean, ci_low, ci_high)

def main():
    records = load_records()
    results = compute_scores(records)
    groups = group_stats(results)

    print("Group\tType\tMean\tCI_low\tCI_high\tN")
    for label, vals in groups.items():
        for kind in ("method","moral"):
            arr = vals[kind]
            mean, lo, hi = summarize(arr)
            print(f"{label}\t{kind}\t{mean:.2f}\t{lo:.2f}\t{hi:.2f}\t{len(arr)}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
